1: 'For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem.' The phrase 'restore the fortunes' (shavti et-shevut) is a technical covenant-restoration term (cf. Jeremiah 29:14, Joel 30:3, Deuteronomy 30:3). The phrase literally means 'to turn the captivity' — the great reversal of exile through divine action. The temporal marker 'in those days and at that time' (bayamim hahem uvaaet hahiy) links this to the Day of YHWH events of Joel 2:28-32. The restoration of Judah and Jerusalem is the precondition for the judgment of the nations in vv2-16.
2: 'I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat. And I will enter into judgment with them there, on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel, because they have scattered them among the nations and have divided up my land.' The Valley of Jehoshaphat (ge Yoshafat) means 'YHWH judges' — whether this is an actual valley (traditionally identified with the Kidron Valley between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives) or a symbolic name is debated. The Kidron Valley association became strong in Jewish and Christian tradition; the Islamic tradition also places the judgment there. The charges against the nations: scattering (pizru) Israel among the nations and dividing (chaleku) YHWH's land. These are political and territorial crimes against both the people and the covenant land.
3: 'And have cast lots for my people, and have traded a boy for a prostitute, and have sold a girl for wine and have drunk it.' The slave trade described here — children sold for wine and sexual services — reflects the practice of enslaving captured populations and selling individuals to traders. The 'casting lots' (yadu goral) for people was the standard distribution of war captives among victorious soldiers (cf. Nahum 3:10, where Nineveh's children are treated the same way). Selling a boy for the price of a prostitute, a girl for a cup of wine — the devaluation of human beings to the price of pleasure goods is the specific charge against the nations.
4: 'What are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia? Are you paying me back for something? If you are paying me back, I will return your payment on your own head swiftly and speedily.' Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia are the coastal neighbors who specifically profited from the slave trade: Phoenician (Tyrian) merchants controlled the Mediterranean slave trade, while Philistines had long been Israel's land-based enemy. Amos 1:6, 9 makes identical charges: Amos condemns Gaza (Philistia) for selling whole populations to Edom, and Tyre for delivering entire communities to Edom. The rhetorical question 'what are you to me?' (mah-attem li) challenges them to justify their actions.
5: 'Because you have taken my silver and my gold, and have carried my rich treasures into your temples.' 'My silver and my gold' — YHWH owns the treasures of Israel, and their removal to foreign temples (cf. 1 Kings 14:26: Shishak of Egypt carrying off the temple treasury) is theft from YHWH himself. The specific destination — 'your temples' (heikhaloteihem) — means the Phoenician and Philistine cult temples received the consecrated objects of YHWH's sanctuary. This is both robbery and sacrilege: pagan temples housing the holy vessels of YHWH is an offense to the divine presence.
6: 'You have sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks, so as to remove them far from their own border.' The Greeks here are the Ionians (Yavanim) — the term for Greeks in the OT (Genesis 10:2, Isaiah 66:19, Ezekiel 27:13). The Phoenician cities of Tyre and Sidon were active in the Greek slave trade in the Eastern Mediterranean. The selling of Judean captives 'far from their border' — into the Greek world where return would be nearly impossible — is the specific geographical dimension of the punishment: they removed Israel from its covenant land, so YHWH will use Israel to remove them from their power base.
7: 'Behold, I will stir them up from the place to which you have sold them, and I will return your payment on your own head.' The divine reversal: YHWH himself will 'stir up' (me'ir) the sold slaves from wherever they have been scattered. This becomes the basis for the eschatological ingathering theme — the Diaspora Jews scattered among the nations will be gathered back. The retaliation formula 'return your payment on your own head' (hashivoti gemulchem bero'shechem) is the lex talionis applied at the national level: what they did to Israel will be done to them.
8: 'I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hand of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans, to a nation far away, for the LORD has spoken.' The Sabeans (Sheba) were the people of modern Yemen (Arabia Felix) — famous in the ancient world for their trading networks (1 Kings 10:1-13, the Queen of Sheba). The selling of Tyrian and Philistine children to the Sabeans mirrors exactly what they did to Judean children: long-distance slave trade with no return. 'For the LORD has spoken' (ki YHWH diber) — the prophetic authorization formula concludes the oracle.
9: 'Proclaim this among the nations: Consecrate for war; stir up the mighty men. Let all the men of war draw near; let them come up.' The ironic summons to the nations: come to the valley to fight. This is the opposite of the Isaianic vision (Isaiah 2:4, Micah 4:3: 'beat swords into plowshares'); here it is 'beat plowshares into swords' (v10). The nations are being summoned not to be blessed but to be judged — YHWH as the divine warrior assembles his enemies precisely so he can defeat them comprehensively. The 'consecrate for war' (qadshu milchamah) uses the ritual preparation language for holy war.
10: 'Beat your plowshares into swords, and your pruning hooks into spears; let the weak say, I am a warrior.' The deliberate reversal of Isaiah 2:4 and Micah 4:3 ('they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks') is rhetorically pointed: those peacetime prophecies envisioned an eschatological age of peace; Joel's summons inverts that image to call the nations to the final battle where they will be destroyed. 'Let the weak say, I am a warrior' (yomar challash gibbor ani) — even the weakest nations should come, because no human strength will matter in this judgment; YHWH will defeat them all equally.
11: 'Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather yourselves there. Bring down your warriors, O LORD.' The gathering of the nations to the valley — and then the petition to YHWH: 'bring down your warriors (gibborim), O LORD' — meaning the heavenly host that YHWH will deploy against the assembled nations. This inverts the nations' expectation: they come as warriors but encounter YHWH's heavenly warriors. The divine army concept (the host of heaven as YHWH's military force) appears in Joshua 5:14-15 (commander of YHWH's army at Jericho), 2 Kings 6:17 (horses and chariots of fire), and Revelation 19:14 (the armies of heaven).
12: 'Let the nations stir themselves up and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.' YHWH sitting to judge (eshevu lishpot) in the Valley of Jehoshaphat — the image of the divine judge enthroned in the valley between the nations. The 'surrounding nations' (goyim missaviv) — all nations that encircle and threaten Israel. The ancient Near Eastern concept of divine judgment involved the deity enthroned with the assembly of divine beings and human representatives; Joel draws on this imagery for the eschatological court.
13: 'Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in, tread, for the winepress is full. The vats overflow, for their evil is great.' The harvest and winepress as images of divine judgment — the nations are the crop ripe for harvest, the grape cluster pressed in the winepress of wrath. This imagery is developed extensively in Revelation 14:14-20 (the harvest of the earth and the winepress of God's wrath, which 'was trodden outside the city, and blood flowed from the winepress, as high as a horse's bridle'). 'Their evil is great' (ki rabbah ra'atam) — the evil has reached the full measure that triggers divine judgment (cf. Genesis 15:16: 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete').
14: 'Multitudes, multitudes, in the valley of decision! For the day of the LORD is near in the valley of decision.' The 'valley of decision' (ge hacharuts) — charuts means a sharp instrument (threshing sledge), or decisiveness/determination. The repeated 'multitudes, multitudes' (hamonim hamonim) is an emphatic double — the assembly of all nations in this valley for the decisive moment of divine judgment. The entire book has been building to this: the locust plague (Joel 1) → the Day of YHWH approaching (Joel 2:1-11) → the call to repentance (Joel 2:12-17) → the Spirit outpouring (Joel 2:28-32) → the gathering of nations to judgment (Joel 3).
15: 'The sun and the moon are darkened, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The cosmic signs from 2:10 and 2:31 recur — the final Day of YHWH is accompanied by the darkening of all heavenly lights. This creates an inclusio with the cosmic signs of Joel 2: the same phenomena that accompanied the locust-army now accompany the final judgment. The darkening of the cosmos represents the absolute sovereignty of the divine event: all natural light-sources yield to the theophanic presence. In the NT, the darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45) and the eschatological darkness of the Apocalypse (Revelation 6:12) draw on this tradition.
16: 'The LORD roars from Zion, and utters his voice from Jerusalem, and the heavens and the earth quake. But the LORD is a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the people of Israel.' The divine roar from Zion (YHWH mizion yish'ag) parallels Amos 1:2 exactly: both books use the same formula. The shaking heavens and earth recall Joel 2:10. But the pivot: the very cosmic event that terrifies the nations is the refuge of YHWH's people. The 'stronghold' (maoz) image — YHWH as fortress of Israel — is the foundation of Ps 46 ('God is our refuge and strength... therefore we will not fear though the earth gives way'). Divine wrath and divine refuge are simultaneously expressed in the same event.
17: 'So you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain. And Jerusalem shall be holy, and strangers shall never again pass through it.' The covenant knowledge formula — 'you shall know that I am the LORD your God' — echoes 2:27 and completes the epistemological arc of the book. YHWH 'dwelling in Zion' (shochen betzion har qodshi) — the Zion theology of the Psalms (Pss 46, 48, 76): Jerusalem as YHWH's dwelling place, inviolable by enemies. 'Strangers shall never again pass through' (zarim lo-ya'avru vah od) — the final security of the holy city, end of the era of conquest and desecration.
18: 'And in that day the mountains shall drip sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk, and all the stream beds of Judah shall flow with water; and a fountain shall come forth from the house of the LORD and water the Valley of Shittim.' The eschatological abundance: mountains dripping wine, hills flowing milk, riverbeds full of water. These were proverbially used in descriptions of Canaan's fertility (Exodus 3:8, 'flowing with milk and honey'). 'A fountain from the house of the LORD' (uma'yan mibbeit YHWH yetze) — a life-giving spring from the temple itself: this becomes the great eschatological image of Ezekiel 47:1-12 (the river from the temple) and Revelation 22:1-2 (the river of the water of life from the throne of God). The Valley of Shittim (modern Jordan Valley) — notoriously dry — will be watered.
19: 'Egypt shall become a desolation and Edom a desolate wilderness, for the violence done to the people of Judah, because they have shed innocent blood in their land.' Egypt and Edom are the classic enemies of Judah — Egypt as the ancient oppressor (the Exodus) and Edom as the perpetual rival (descended from Esau, who sold his birthright; the Edomites celebrated when Jerusalem fell, Psalms 137:7, Obad 10-14). 'Innocent blood' (dam-naki) — the specific charge of blood-guilt that cries to YHWH (cf. Genesis 4:10, Abel's blood). Their desolation is explicitly punitive — not arbitrary but for specific covenant crimes against YHWH's people.
20: 'But Judah shall be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem to all generations.' The eternal habitation of Judah and Jerusalem — the covenantal promise of permanent settlement. Against the desolation of Egypt and Edom (v19), Judah and Jerusalem will be permanently inhabited. This is the Zion theology's ultimate expression: the eternal city, the inviolable dwelling of YHWH's people. In the NT, this finds its eschatological form in the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2-3: 'I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven... and I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man').
21: 'I will avenge their blood, blood I have not avenged, for the LORD dwells in Zion.' The closing verse takes up the blood-guilt theme from v19: YHWH declares he will avenge 'blood I have not avenged' (venikkeiti damam lo-nikkeiti) — the long-deferred justice finally executed. 'For the LORD dwells in Zion' (vaYHWH shochen betzion) — the book ends where it is most rooted: YHWH's presence in Zion is both the guarantee of Israel's protection and the basis of his ultimate judicial authority. The dwelling of YHWH in Zion is the eschatological foundation that makes all the promises possible.
